title: NPU FP16 compute parallelism PnR stability
hypothesis: The single-sample nm32 timing result can look better than nm16 because OpenROAD placement and routing quality varies by random seed. Running seed trials across nm8, nm16, and nm32 will distinguish a real scaling trend from PnR-quality noise before choosing nm64 or changing dispatcher architecture.

direct_comparison.primary_question: How stable are critical path, power, and implementation runtime across OpenROAD seeds as FP16 GEMM module count increases from nm8 to nm32?
include: full npu_top wrapper with the same cmp33 floorplan and NPU shell used by the compute parallelism ladder
nm8, nm16, and nm32 FP16 GEMM module counts
three control-plane trials with FLOW_RANDOM_SEED propagated to OpenROAD
exclude: nm64 boundary measurement before nm16/nm32 stability is understood
memory hierarchy, NoC, or producer scheduling changes

expected_benefit: quantifies whether the apparent nm32 timing improvement over nm16 is within seed variance
provides mean, best, worst, and spread for the current centralized dynamic dispatcher
sets a more reliable basis for deciding whether to run nm64 or pivot to a hierarchical/static scheduler
risks: three seeds are enough for a first confidence check but not a full statistical characterization
the fixed cmp33 floorplan may dominate variance at larger module counts
result aggregation must keep flat and hier modes separated while comparing only seed changes

expected_result.reason: Use seed variance to decide whether nm32 is genuinely no worse than nm16, and whether nm64 is a meaningful next physical point.
